headline: Ugandan Coffee Farmers Find Hope in Ancient Soil Science
meta_description: In Uganda's Masaka region, smallholder coffee farmers are transforming their yields through regenerative agriculture, proving resilience against drought.
summary_short: Ugandan coffee farmers in Masaka are harvesting stronger crops and better incomes by adopting regenerative farming techniques that heal drought-stressed soil.
body:
In Uganda's Masaka region, coffee farmer Nakalisa Mary Fatuma watched her harvests shrivel as drought and erratic rains became routine threats. Then everything changed.

Through a coalition involving the Global Environment Facility, Nespresso, and conservation groups, 30 demonstration farms were established across Masaka to teach local growers a forgotten approach: regenerative agriculture. The method sounds simple—mulching, cover cropping, shade trees—but its effects run deep.

"Too little or untimely rains have become serious threats to coffee production," Fatuma explained. "But since we applied mulches and planted drought resistant seedlings, the coffee farms are reliably resilient."

Regenerative farming differs from organic farming in a crucial way: it doesn't just grow crops, it grows the topsoil itself. By never tilling the earth and layering organic material through mulch and complementary plants, farmers rebuild soil's capacity to retain water and nutrients. Shade trees add further protection while their fallen leaves enrich the ground.

The results have spread through Masaka like ripples. Farmers report not just improved yields and quality, but healthier trees and stable income. "We used to think erosion was just something we had to live with," farmer Munanira Joseph said. "But when we saw how the soil stayed in place on the demo plot, everyone wanted to try."

As global coffee prices continue climbing, these Ugandan farmers show that resilience grows from the ground up.
